Gothitelle.overview: Although nowhere near as bulky as the metagame's premier Psychic-type, Cresselia, Shadow Tag gives Gothitelle an incredible niche in the format. With it, Gothitelle can tactically pin and eliminate key threats to its team's endgame as well as enabling the Perish trap archetype in its entirety. Gothitelle, however, is a one trick pony, as its offensive movepool is not deep enough to leverage and its natural bulk is not sufficient enough to invest into its modest Special Attack; both of these are true for Cresselia.

Shadow Tag Trapper.description:
• Gothitelle's main goal is to stay on the field and provide whatever support it can to its teammates. To this end, defensive moves like Rest and Protect are necessary to keep it alive and keep opposing Pokemon trapped.
  • Psychic and either Trick Room or Thunder Wave give Gothitelle the ability to exert some pressure on the field by threatening chip damage or speed control.
  • Gothitelle's EVs are dumped into its bulk, allowing it to comfortably live most major hits like Dragon Gem Draco Meteor from Latios and then recover back with Rest.

Other Options

  • Skill Swap lets Gothitelle pass its ability to its partner, creating potentially unwinnable game states for the opponent as they stare down a Shadow Tag Ferrothorn or Suicune. Both of these Pokemon are naturally very bulky, have setup moves, and can be impossible walls to break through if the right opposing Pokemon are trapped.
  • Gothitelle gets numerous interesting support moves, such as Safeguard, Charm, and Tickle, to support its team.
  • Other items like Sitrus Berry, Leftovers, and Lum Berry can also fit on Gothitelle as needed and allow it to run the aforementioned support moves.

Common Partners

  • Scrafty supports Gothitelle by threatening one of its biggest threats in Tyranitar, mitigating the damage from Landorus-T through Intimidate, and aiding in Trick Room going up with Fake Out.
  • A Perish Song user, most commonly Politoed, is required for a Perish trap team.

Checks and Counters

  • Thundurus is not threatened by Gothitelle and can use Taunt to prevent it from setting up Trick Room or paralyzing its partner.
  • Dark-types such as Tyranitar and Scrafty are immune to Gothitelle's Psychic and 2HKO with Crunch.
  • Landorus-T nearly 2HKOes Gothitelle with U-turn, which also switches itself out of the effects of Perish Song.
  • Volcarona, particularly Bug Gem sets, is capable of OHKOing Gothitelle and eliminating it as a potential threat.

Volcarona.overview: Due to its unique typing, Volcarona is a dangerous anti-metagame option. Its Bug- and Fire-type moves super effectively hit the ubiquitous Steel- and Psychic-type Pokemon such as Metagross and Cresselia, as well as Amoonguss. While Volcarona's typing makes it an offensive threat, its typing can just as easily make it a defensive liability. Volcarona is 4x weak to the ever prevalent Rock Slide, which is often used by Choice Scarf Pokemon such as Landorus-T and Tyranitar or on teams with dedicated speed control. This leads to Volcarona having polarizing matchups; it is likely to check a few Pokemon on the opponent's team and be checked by the rest.

Quiver Dance Sweeper.description:
• This set aims to sweep teams with a Quiver Dance, which makes an already potent offensive threat all the more deadly.
  • After a Quiver Dance, Bug Buzz lets Volcarona potentially OHKO specially bulkier threats such as Cresselia, Tyranitar, and Rotom-W depending on their EV spread.
  • Lum Berry is all but required in order to buy it an extra turn against Thundurus's Prankster Thunder Wave.
  • 44 HP and 4 Defense EVs let Volcarona survive a Rock Slide from Adamant Landorus-T that has been affected by Intimidate, and 244 Speed EVs let it outspeed Choice Scarf Hydreigon after a Quiver Dance. The remaining EVs are dumped into Special Attack.

Other Options

  • Overheat lets Volcarona OHKO Amoonguss. Bug Gem leverages Bug Buzz’s great neutral coverage and turns it into a deadly attack, with the ability to OHKO offensive Cresselia sets and deal heavy damage to Rotom-Wash.
  • Bulkier sets with Rage Powder and Lum Berry let Volcarona support its team in a variety of ways, leveraging its Flame Body ability against Fake Out users and being a superb answer to Amoonguss.
  • Giga Drain can potentially flip matchups against bulky Water-types such as Rotom-Wash, Jellicent, and Politoed while giving Volcarona some longevity.

Common Partners

  • Hitmontop is the quintessential partner for Volcarona, especially if it's using a Quiver Dance set. Hitmontop provides Intimidate support to offset its partner's abysmal Defense stat, while its Fake Out support enables Volcarona to get off a Quiver Dance and then deal significant damage, which can be potentially bolstered with Feint or Helping Hand.
  • Choice Scarf Landorus-T checks or threatens most of Volcarona's threats, although it exacerbates the team's weakness to rain teams.

Checks and Counters

  • Heatran is resistant to Bug and is immune to Volcarona's Fire-type attacks, letting it switch in with impunity and threaten Volcarona with its Fire-type attacks or use Substitute.
  • Rain teams greatly reduce Volcarona's damage potential, and rain sweepers like Kingdra will easily OHKO it.
  • Tornadus outspeeds and OHKOes it with Acrobatics.
  • Thundurus's Thunder Wave turns Volcarona into a sitting duck.
  • What checks Volcarona is also largely dependent on its EV spread. Latios outspeeds an unboosted Volcarona and can OHKO non-bulky sets with Dragon Gem Draco Meteor. Slower sets without proper defensive investment can find themselves OHKOed by a Draco Meteor from a speedy Hydreigon. Landorus-T, particularly Choice Scarf variants, are also a major threat; Rock Slide OHKOes Volcarona unless Landorus-T has been hit by Intimidate, and Stone Edge OHKOes it even at -1.

Heatran.overview: Heatran is not only the premier Fire-type Pokemon of the VGC 2013 metagame but also one of the best Steel-types as well. It utilizes its numerous resistances and great bulk to switch in on the pervasive Psychic-, Steel-, and Dragon-type Pokemon of the format and threaten with its impressive Special Attack and access to the powerful spread moves Heat Wave and Eruption. Although Heatran's typing does it a lot of good, it does render it weak to three very common attacking types in Fighting, Water, and Ground, which makes positioning Heatran all the more important.

Substitute.description:
• Earth Power's main target is opposing Heatran, letting Heatran beat any slower Heatran sets without Shuca Berry. It also gives Heatran a way to hit Fire-resistant foes not named Rotom-Wash, Hydreigon, and Latios.
  • Substitute and Leftovers leverage Heatan's fantastic defensive typing and natural bulk to regularly switch in, set up a Substitute on a forced switch, and threaten the opposing team from behind the Substitute.
  • A Timid nature and 204 Speed EVs allow Heatran to outspeed Breloom, letting it set up a Substitute in front of an otherwise very threatening Pokemon. 204 HP EVs maximize the recovery given by Leftovers. The remaining EVs were dumped in Special Attack, though they can be distributed into Special Defense to survive an Earth Power from an opposing Timid Heatran.

ErupTran (Eruption).description:
• Fire Gem-boosted Eruption from Heatran is the strongest spread special attack in the format. Heat Wave gives Heatran a strong non-conditional STAB spread option once it has taken damage. Earth Power is selected mainly to OHKO opposing Heatran not holding a Shuca Berry.
  • Eruption Heatran is locked to a Quiet nature, and this set has opted for minimum Speed to be a Trick Room sweeper.

Other Options

  • Shuca Berry allows Heatran to survive an Earthquake from Landorus-T or Earth Power from opposing Heatran.
  • Hidden Power Grass gives Heatran a way to beat Gastrodon, which would otherwise OHKO Heatran with Earth Power.
  • Fire Gem-boosted Overheat is likely to OHKO bulky Thundurus and OHKOes Conkeldurr.
  • A plethora of EV spreads and Speed stats are viable. For example, Eruption Heatran can run 20 Speed EVs with 31 Speed IVs to outspeed Latios and Tornadus while under Tailwind.

Common Partners

  • Cresselia and Heatran have incredible synergy. Cresselia not only checks some of Heatran's biggest threats in Landorus-T and Conkeldurr but also has access to a large number of supporting tools that enable Heatran, such as Sunny Day, Skill Swap, and Trick Room.
  • Thundurus checks pesky Water-type Pokemon and debilitates faster threats with Thunder Wave, allowing even the slowest Heatran to move first.
  • Tornadus OHKOes Landorus-T and the Fighting-types that threaten Heatran and can provide speed control in the form of Tailwind, while Heatran removes the Steel-types that Tornadus cannot reliably hit such as Metagross and opposing Heatran.

Checks and Counters

  • Ground-types, such as Landorus-T, greatly threaten even Shuca Berry Heatran sets with their powerful STAB Earthquake.
  • Other Heatran are powered up by Heatran's Fire-type attacks and threaten with an Earth Power of their own.
  • Bulky Water-types all check Heatran in a variety of ways. All of them threaten Heatran with their STAB moves and resist Heatran's own, and some, like Rotom-Wash, Suicune, and Politoed, can mitigate Heatran's damage output with Light Screen, Snarl, and Drizzle, respectively.
  • Fighting-types, particularly Hitmontop and Conkeldurr, are capable of OHKOing Heatran.

Magmar.overview: Magmar is an unconventional redirector that first saw usage at the 2013 World Championships, famously used by future world champions Sejun Park & Wolfe Glick. Although it can't use Vital Spirit to stand up against Amoonguss, Flame Body gives Magmar more value against physical attackers that make contact, such as the Fake Out users Hitmontop and Scrafty, Tornadus, and U-turn Landorus-T. Eviolite transforms Magmar's mediocre defensive stats into something to be marveled, and its Special Attack stat enables it to threaten common Steel-types and Amoonguss without much investment. Unlike other redirectors, Magmar is vulnerable to spread attacks, such as the ubiquitous Earthquake and Rock Slide, as well as Muddy Water. Magmar is also item-locked and cannot hold a Mental Herb or Lum Berry, limiting its options against Taunt and status users like Thundurus and Amoonguss.

Bulky Redirector.description:
• The attacking option for Magmar is largely inconsequential. Overheat does the most burst damage and can threaten an even slightly weakened Metagross or Amoonguss, while Flamethrower and Heat Wave let Magmar deal more consistent damage over multiple turns. Flamethrower lets Magmar 2HKO Metagross and gives it a reasonable chance to 2HKO Amoonguss, while Heat Wave trades single-target damage for spread chip damage and twice as many chances at a burn.
  • Will-O-Wisp lets Magmar reliably burn the myriad of threatening physical attackers of the metagame, such as Conkeldurr, Metagross, Tornadus, and Tyranitar.
  • The HP and Defense EVs ensure Magmar survives a Hammer Arm from Life Orb Conkeldurr. The 76 Speed EVs let Magmar outspeed Adamant Hitmontop, allowing Magmar to redirect Hitmontop's Fake Out, protect its partner, and potentially burn Hitmontop. The remaining EVs are dumped into Special Defense, which lets Magmar always survives Dragon Gem Latios's Draco Meteor and Life Orb Kingdra's rain-boosted Muddy Water.

Other Options

  • Confuse Ray lets Magmar disrupt attackers in a different, albeit more RNG dependent, way.
  • 84 Special Attack EVs let Magmar OHKO maximum HP Metagross with Overheat.

Common Partners

  • Offensive Jellicent deals with two of Magmar's biggest threats in Landorus-T and Heatran, while Magmar helps Jellicent set up Trick Room.
  • Dragon-types like Hydreigon and Latios synergize well with Magmar. Magmar redirects threatening attacks from Tornadus and deals with Metagross, while the Dragon-types can threaten Heatran and bulky Water-types, which threaten Magmar.

Checks and Counters

  • Water-types such as Suicune, Kingdra, and Rotom-Wash all give Magmar a hard time, especially if they're on rain teams. Magmar is unable to meaningfully damage these Pokemon and does not want to redirect a super effective Water-type attack to itself.
  • Magmar is unable to do anything to Heatran, which can 2HKO Magmar with Earth Power.
  • Landorus-T deals considerable damage with Earthquake.

Tyranitar.overview: Tyranitar is a staple of the VGC 2013 metagame. Its incredible stat spread gives it fantastic natural bulk and even better offensive potential. One of Tyranitar's biggest assets is its ability, Sand Stream, which breaks Focus Sashes and potentially puts Pokemon into KO range of subsequent attacks while also bolstering its own Special Defense which lets it stand up against strong Special Attackers like Latios and Thundurus-T. Tyranitar's typing can be polarizing, but it has several relevant resistances and an immunity. In addition, Tyranitar's two STAB moves hit several top-tier threats for super effective damage, such as Cresselia, Thundurus, and Volcarona. Because of these attributes, Tyranitar will likely threaten several Pokemon on the opponent's team and be threatened by the rest.

Choice Scarf.description:
• Rock Slide is Tyranitar's preferred Rock-type STAB move, dealing considerable spread damage and having the chance to flinch foes.
  • Stone Edge OHKOes bulky Thundurus.
  • Low Kick hits Heatran, Hydreigon, and opposing Tyranitar harder, while Ice Punch lets Tyranitar catch a Landorus-T switching in for a KO.
  • Choice Scarf Tyranitar is designed to catch the opponent by surprise and pick up a turn 1 OHKO on a major fast threat the opponent presumed was safe.
  • Maximum Speed investment is required for Tyranitar to outspeed Latios and Tornadus and OHKO them with its STAB moves.

Bulky Attacker.description:
• Rock Slide is Tyranitar's preferred Rock-type STAB move, as it deals great damage and, when paired with any form of speed control, can fish for flinches.
  • Crunch lets Tyranitar dispose of cornerstones of the metagame such as Cresselia and Latios while hitting Metagross, Rotom-Wash, and others for neutral damage.
  • Low Kick gives Tyranitar a better matchup against Hydreigon, Heatran, and opposing Tyranitar.
  • The defensive EVs allow it to survive a Meteor Mash from maximum Attack Adamant Metagross. The remaining EVs are dumped into Attack.
  • Bulky Tyranitar can consider running minimum Speed investment to underspeed Politoed and ensure sand is the active field effect in a weather war.
  • Chople Berry lets Tyranitar survive non-STAB Fighting-type coverage attacks, such as a Superpower from Landorus-T and Tornadus. A Fighting Gem-boosted Close Combat from Hitmontop or a Life Orb-boosted Hammer Arm from Conkeldurr with still OHKO Tyranitar through the Chople Berry, however.

Other Options

  • Fire Punch or Ice Punch can be used to hit Metagross or Landorus-T, respectively.
  • Fling + Iron Ball is capable of OHKOing Cresselia.

Common Partners

  • Amoonguss resists Water and Fighting for Tyranitar, while Tyranitar dispatches the Fire-, Flying-, and Psychic-types that threaten Amoonguss.
  • Thundurus makes an excellent partner for Tyranitar. Thunder Wave lets even slow bulky sets move first and fish for Rock Slide flinches. Thundurus with Hidden Power Ice threatens Landorus-T and Water-types that can KO Tyranitar.

Checks and Counters

  • Conkeldurr OHKOes non-Chople Berry sets with priority Mach Punch and resists both of Tyranitar's STAB moves.
  • Metagross resists Rock Slide and can OHKO even bulky sets with Meteor Mash.
  • Landorus-T threatens Tyranitar with Earthquake and lowers its damage output with Intimidate.

Breloom.overview: Breloom's high Attack; workable Speed stat that allows it to outspeed top threats such as Cresselia, Metagross, and many Heatran sets; and access to Spore make it a threat to many Pokemon. Unlike its defensive Spore-spamming counterpart in Amoonguss, Breloom is an offensive threat to be respected. If unaccounted for in the builder or left unchecked, it can overpower teams. Due to its severe item limitation, Breloom is far more susceptible to counterplay, be it Taunt or a tandem of fast attackers.

Offensive Spore.description:
• Bullet Seed and Mach Punch deal significant damage to a surprising number of common Pokemon. Mach Punch OHKOes common Choice Scarf Tyranitar sets, while Bullet Seed almost always OHKOes Rotom-Wash through Sitrus Berry with just three hits.
  • Focus Sash ensures Breloom can attempt to use Spore at least once.
  • Maximum Speed EVs allow Breloom to use Spore on as many Pokemon as possible before they are capable of attacking.
  • Protect allows Breloom to stay on the field as long as possible. Breloom draws a lot of attention to itself by virtue of its threatening kit, and it can steal a foe's turn with a timely Protect just as easily as it can by taking advantage of Gen 5 sleep mechanics.

Other Options

  • A Choice Scarf allows Breloom to put significantly faster targets to sleep before they can act, such as Latios and Tornadus.
  • A Fighting Gem set with Superpower gives Breloom an unexpectedly powerful attack option, with a chance to OHKO bulky targets like Conkeldurr and Rotom-Wash, at the cost of the longevity provided by Focus Sash.

Common Partners

  • Tornadus supports Breloom with Tailwind and OHKOes Latios, which threatens Breloom. Other Tailwind users like Suicune and Togekiss can perform a similar function. None of these partners are capable of threatening Thundurus, however, which is a major threat to Breloom.
  • Liepard supports Breloom with Fake Out and Encore, punishing foes that use Protect to block Spore.

Checks and Counters

  • Thundurus with Taunt prevents Breloom from putting its partners to sleep, and it resists both of Breloom's STAB moves.
  • Fast Substitute users, such as Heatran, can freely set up in front of Breloom and prevent it from using Spore on them.
  • Because Breloom is so frail and heavily relies on Spore, it is susceptible to being caught off guard by an unexpected Lum Berry holder that absorbs the sleep and can KO Breloom in response.
  • Similarly, Lum Berry holders with redirecting moves such as Amoonguss and Volcarona draw the Spore to itself and allow their partner to freely attack Breloom.
  • Although unreliable, an untimely one-turn sleep can often be the end of a potential Breloom sweep.

Metagross.overview: Metagross is consistently one of the most used Pokemon in the format, and for many valid reasons. A Steel typing gives Metagross several valuable resistances, which are enhanced by its excellent natural bulk. In addition, Metagross has a diverse enough movepool to threaten a multitude of Pokemon with its monstrous base 135 Attack. Because of these traits, there are few games where Metagross feels like a bad selection. Although Metagross's two STAB types combine for near-perfect neutral coverage, individually they are resisted by some of the most common typings in the format. As such, Metagross may find itself regularly attacking switch-ins that resist its selected move.

Physical Attacker.description:
• Meteor Mash is Metagross's strongest Steel-type attacking move and preferred option, hitting key KO ranges on threats like Latios and Tyranitar. In addition, triggering its secondary effect can easily turn a game on its head.
  • Zen Headbutt hits more targets for neutral damage than Meteor Mash, and it most importantly hits key threats like Amoonguss and Conkeldurr for super effective damage.
  • Bullet Punch guarantees damage before Metagross gets KOed, as well as finishing off low-HP Pokemon. The combination of non-Steel Gem Meteor Mash and Bullet Punch can KO Latios.
  • Earthquake is a secondary option that gives Metagross a way to hit opposing Metagross and potentially flip the matchup on Heatran.
  • Psychic Gem lets Metagross OHKO Amoonguss with Zen Headbutt, Steel Gem lets it OHKO Latios and Tyranitar with Meteor Mash, and Lum Berry prevents status effects or enables its partner to use Swagger on Metagross.
  • With maximum HP investment, Metagross is only 3HKOed by opposing Metagross's Earthquake.

Other Options

  • Iron Head is sometimes chosen as an alternative to Meteor Mash because it cannot miss and can make the target flinch if it moves after Metagross.
  • Ice Punch can be used to hit the numerous top-tier threats weak to Ice, such as Landorus-T, Thundurus, Latios, and Amoonguss.
  • Substitute + Leftovers is a threatening set, as Metagross forces many switches and can take advantage to position itself better.
  • Shuca Berry or Occa Berry allows Metagross to flip one of its bad matchups in Landorus-T and Heatran, respectively. Doing so requires slotting in Ice Punch or Earthquake, however.
  • Investing EVs into Metagross's Speed can be useful, but there is no standard amount to allocate. Outspeeding uninvested Cresselia and Rotom formes is worth considering, but this comes at the cost of the power and bulk of Metagross.

Common Partners

  • Rotom-Wash + Metagross is a tried and true pairing, with Rotom-Wash threatening both Heatran and Landorus-T and Metagross eliminating Amoonguss and Latios for Rotom-Wash.
  • Cresselia can support Metagross in numerous ways. It can use Swagger on a Lum Berry set; provide speed control via Trick Room, Icy Wind, or Thunder Wave; threaten Landorus-T with Ice Beam or Heatran with Hidden Power Ground; and even give Levitate to Metagross with Skill Swap.
  • Thundurus can support Metagross similarly to Cresselia but with far less variety, being limited to just Thunder Wave, Swagger, and Hidden Power Ice.

Checks and Counters

  • Heatran resists both STAB moves and 2HKOes Metagross with Heat Wave. It can also freely set up Substitute in front of Metagross.
  • Rain teams pose a significant threat to Metagross, as it is nearly OHKOed by a rain-boosted Muddy Water from Kingdra and does not meaningfully threaten Kingdra back with Zen Headbutt.
  • Landorus-T survives the combination of any non-Gem-boosted attack and Bullet Punch and 2HKOes Metagross with Earthquake.

Latios.overview: Latios is the format's defining special attacker, with opposing EV spreads crafted specifically to survive its powerful STAB Draco Meteor. There are few games where Latios cannot meaningfully contribute because its base stats are highly optimized for its role as an offensive sweeper, enabled further by its fantastic movepool, strong offensive typing, and a useful ability. However, because Latios's presence has shaped the metagame so heavily, every team must have a plethora of answers for it.

Special Sweeper.description:
• Dragon Gem Draco Meteor is among the strongest single-target attacks in the format, dealing upwards of half to resists such as Metagross and Heatran and potentially OHKOing specially bulky targets it hits for neutral damage such as Volcarona and Rotom-Wash.
  • Psychic and Psyshock are powerful secondary STAB options: Psychic deals more to Conkeldurr than Psyshock, while Psyshock hits specially bulky Pokemon like Rotom-Wash and Amoonguss marginally harder than Psychic.
  • Latios's third move is highly flexible and can be whatever the team needs. Thunderbolt gives Latios a strong attack for Metagross and Heatran as well as against common Water-types like Suicune and Jellicent without Draco Meteor's drawbacks. Hidden Power Ground can be used to hit common Draco Meteor switch-ins like Heatran and Metagross for super effective damage. Substitute capitalizes on a forced switch or Protect from the opponent and provides Latios with some protection from an incoming attack.

Other Options

  • Life Orb gives Latios more consistent power at the cost of losing its format-defining nuclear bomb option in Dragon Gem Draco Meteor. Life Orb, however, does let Latios OHKO non-Shuca Berry Heatran with Hidden Power Ground and 2HKO Jellicent and bulky Thundurus with Psyshock.
  • A Modest nature gives Latios a reasonable chance to OHKO Pokemon it couldn't with a Timid nature, although it now has to delicately play around opposing Timid Latios, Hydreigon, and Volcarona.
  • Latios has seen success using a diverse number of attacking and supporting moves; provided they sufficiently benefit its partners. A team particularly weak to Gastrodon can use Energy Ball, a team weak to rain can use Sunny Day, and a team lacking speed control can use Tailwind.

Common Partners

  • Landorus-T checks Steel-types for Latios, pairing well with it due to Latios's immunity to Earthquake.
  • Fire-types like Heatran are common partners to check Steel-types and appreciate Latios's ability to cut through the Water-types that threaten it.
  • Tyranitar's residual damage from Sand Stream can easily flip matchups on Pokemon EVed to survive Dragon Gem Draco Meteor, as one turn of sand chip damage gives Latios small odds to OHKO Pokemon like Rotom-Wash and Thundurus.
  • Hitmontop supports Latios with Fake Out, Feint, and Helping Hand while also eliminating potential threats like Tyranitar and Steel-types.

Checks and Counters

  • Tornadus outspeeds and OHKOes Latios with Flying Gem Acrobatics. Once the Flying Gem is expended, however, Tornadus no longer OHKOes Latios and is likely OHKOed in return by a Draco Meteor even without a Dragon Gem.
  • Most Thundurus sets survive Dragon Gem Draco Meteor and many players feel it is worth trading the majority of Thundurus's health to paralyze Latios, depriving the dragon of its two most important features: its Dragon Gem and Speed. Common partners like Landorus-T and Tyranitar can swiftly dispose of Thundurus, while Hitmontop takes advantage of bulky Thundurus seldom having Protect to Fake Out in tandem with Latios's Draco Meteor for a KO.
  • Metagross resists both of Latios's STAB moves, is not sufficiently threatened by any of its coverage moves, and 2HKOes Latios with Meteor Mash into Bullet Punch. However, Metagross takes slightly more than half from Latios's Dragon Gem-boosted Draco Meteor and may find itself unable to stand toe-to-toe late-game.

Mamoswine.overview: Mamoswine has been an excellent anti-metagame option since 2012. It has among the best offensive typing in the format, as Ground and Ice hits more Pokemon effectively than any other combination of two types. They also cover each other quite well, as Pokemon that are immune to Ground, such as Thundurus and Latios, are often weak to Ice, and Pokemon that resist Ice are usually weak to Ground, such as Metagross and Heatran. Mamoswine also has a favorable stat distribution, as it is faster than metagame staples like Heatran, Cresselia, and Metagross; bulky enough to withstand strong neutral attacks such as Tornadus's Flying Gem-boosted Acrobatics and Heatran's Heat Wave; and strong enough to pick up key KOs on threats like Amoonguss and Heatran. A drawback of Mamoswine is that its damage output is often lackluster against targets it hits only for neutral damage, such as Cresselia, Conkeldurr, and Suicune. In addition, Mamoswine is hit super effectively by the two most common priority attacks in the format: Mach Punch and Bullet Punch.

Standard Offensive.description:
• Icicle Spear is chosen over Icicle Crash for its reliability and generally higher damage output, especially when it hits four or five times. In addition, its multi-hit nature circumvents Focus Sash and Yache Berry, which are commonly held by Landorus-T.
  • Ice Shard lets Mamoswine pick up KOs on low-HP Pokemon, which can help Icicle Spear htis only two times.
  • Choice Scarf sets should drop Protect for a coverage move such as Rock Slide and can consider dropping Ice Shard for Icicle Crash for the flinch chance.

Other Options

  • A bulky Choice Scarf variant with the EV spread 124 HP / 44 Atk / 100 Def / 44 SpD / 196 Spe was used to win Worlds in 2013, chosen to swiftly eliminate threats like Thundurus and Heatran, which threatened the Tornadus and Latios on the winner's team, while maximizing Mamoswine's good natural bulk. Defensively, this spread lets Mamoswine survive a Superpower from Adamant Scarf Landorus-T and any non-Gem-boosted attack from Timid Latios, and it almost always survives an Overheat from Timid Volcarona. The 44 Attack EVs are still enough to OHKO Thundurus, Latios, and Landorus-T with Icicle Crash, and Heatran with Earthquake.
  • Endeavor synergizes with Focus Sash to bring targets that Mamoswine otherwise could not threaten, such as Suicune and Rotom-Wash, down to 1 HP.
  • Substitute can let Mamoswine exploit its ability to force switches to better position itself against an incoming threat.

Common Partners

  • Thundurus makes an excellent partner for Mamoswine, threatening the bulky Water-types that can wall Mamoswine and potentially threatening Fighting-types with Hidden Power Flying.
  • Strong attackers like Latios and Tornadus cover several of Mamoswine's weaknesses to common Pokemon by muscling through its threats. For example, both are capable of swiftly dealing with Conkeldurr, and Latios greatly threatens bulky Water-types.

Checks and Counters

  • Bulky Water-types, especially Rotom-Wash, resist Mamoswine's Ice-type attacks; take negligible damage from or, in Rotom-Wash's case, are immune to Earthquake; and can easily dispose of Mamoswine with their STAB moves.
  • Conkeldurr deals significant damage with Mach Punch, OHKOes Mamoswine with its other Fighting-type STAB moves, and is more than bulky enough to shrug off Mamoswine's attacks.
  • A Choice Scarf Mamoswine locked into one of its STAB moves can find itself in a position punishable by several Pokemon it would otherwise threaten, such as Heatran, Metagross, and Landorus-T.

Rotom-Wash.overview: Rotom-Wash is in the conversation for being the best bulky Water-type in the format. Due to its typing and ability, Rotom-Wash is only weak to Grass, an offensive typing that's seldomly seen. Its Water- and Electric-type attacks super effectively damage several top threats, such as Suicune, Tornadus, and Heatran, and will hit most things for at least neutral damage. Rotom-Wash has a great stat spread for whatever it needs to do, as not only can it be bulky enough to survive a Draco Meteor from Latios or a Close Combat from Hitmontop, but it can also be strong enough to OHKO major threats such as Heatran and Landorus-T. Although its mediocre Speed may leave something to be desired, it's faster than the majority of physical attackers in the format and can potentially burn them before they get a chance to attack. Although Rotom-Wash is typically bulky enough to survive any single attack, a second one often proves fatal. Due to its middling Speed, many pairs of faster Pokemon, such as Latios, Thundurus, Landorus-T, and Volcarona, can double target and KO Rotom-Wash before it can move. Rotom-Wash also does not typically prevent the opponent from executing their gameplan; a Cresselia is free to use Trick Room and bring in Conkeldurr in front of Rotom-Wash, for example.

Bulky Support.description:
• Thunderbolt is accurate and hits many targets such as Metagross and Thundurus for neutral damage, while Hydro Pump hits some top metagame threats such as Heatran and Tyranitar for super effective damage.
  • Light Screen and Will-O-Wisp allow Rotom-Wash to mitigate damage from special and physical attacks, respectively.
  • The Special Defense investment lets Rotom-Wash survive a Dragon Gem-boosted Draco Meteor from Timid Latios. The remaining EVs are dumped into Defense, giving Rotom-Wash great odds to survive a Hammer Arm from Conkeldurr, but they can be meaningfully allocated into Speed to try and outspeed other moderately slow Pokemon like Cresselia, Heatran, and opposing Rotom-Wash or invested into Special Attack to maximize its chance to OHKO Tornadus with Thunderbolt.

Other Options

  • Rotom-Wash's base 105 Special Attack can be leveraged on an offensive set with either a Gem or Choice item.
  • Rotom-Wash seldom carries Protect, making it prone to being double targeted. Adding Protect can catch opponents off-guard and flip matchups, buying a turn for Rotom-Wash's partner to eliminate a threatening Amoongus, Latios, or Conkeldurr.
  • Rotom-Wash's low base HP makes it an ideal user of Pain Split, which greatly heals off common Pokemon with a high base HP stat such as Cresselia, Amoonguss, and Tyranitar.
  • Reflect allows Rotom-Wash to reduce physical damage taken without having to rely on the often inconsistent Will-O-Wisp.
  • Electroweb lets Rotom-Wash provide speed control for its team.

Common Partners

  • Metagross and Rotom-Wash cover each other's weaknesses, with Metagross removing Amoonguss for Rotom-Wash and Rotom-Wash checking Heatran and Landorus-T for Metagross. They also have perfect typing synergy, as Metagross resists Grass-type attacks, while Rotom-Wash is immune to Ground-type attacks and resists Fire-type attacks.
  • Cresselia and Rotom-Wash have neutral matchups against most leads. The pair can be comfortably lead and trade favorably against common Pokemon like Metagross, Thundurus, and Landorus-T, making for a safe and reliable start to a match.

Checks and Counters

  • Latios resists both of Rotom-Wash's STAB moves and can potentially OHKO it with Draco Meteor, depending on their EV spreads.
  • Amoonguss is not threatened by anything Rotom-Wash can do and can freely use Rage Powder or Spore. Amoonguss also can deal meaningful damage to it with Giga Drain.
  • Thundurus generates a lot of value by targeting Rotom-Wash's position with Thunder Wave, and it often carries Taunt to prevent Rotom-Wash from using Light Screen or Will-O-Wisp.
  • Gastrodon is immune to both of Rotom-Wash's STAB moves and is able to switch in and attack its partner with impunity.

Jellicent.overview: Jellicent's superb typing and natural bulk enable support sets, while its workable Special Attack can be leveraged on Water Gem sets with Water Spout to attempt to sweep teams under Trick Room. Jellicent is the best of the metagame's few viable Ghost-types, and its immunity to both Fake Out and Fighting-type attacks is a valuable asset to an already versatile Pokemon.

Bulky Support.description:
• This set maximizes survivability, with moves like Scald, Will-O-Wisp, and Recover to wear down the opponent's team slowly until it can no longer threaten Jellicent. Scald is a support Jellicent's best friend, as its burn chance cripples physical attackers and deals passive damage.
  • The bulk allows Jellicent to survive two Zen Headbutts from maximum Attack Metagross, a Crunch from Adamant Tyranitar, a Draco Meteor from Dragon Gem Latios, and any two attacks from Kingdra.
  • The Speed EVs let Jellicent outspeed 4 Speed Tyranitar and potentially burn it before it can move.

Trick Room Offensive Sweeper.description:
• Unlike most other Trick Room setters, Jellicent is its own sweeper, allowing its partner to support it as it sets up Trick Room immediately instead of trying to find opportunities to do so over the course of the match.
  • Although its Special Attack is not inspiring, with Water Gem and a powerful spread move in Water Spout, Jellicent becomes a dangerous threat capable of OHKOing Landorus-T and Heatran while 2HKOing Metagross and Tyranitar.
  • Ice Beam and Shadow Ball are secondary attacking options. The former threatens Latios and Tornadus, while the latter threatens Cresselia and other bulky Water-types.
  • A well-positioned Jellicent will find opportunities to use Recover, while Protect is more spammable with less risk.

Other Options

  • Water Absorb is a viable ability, making Jellicent an even better check to rain teams.
  • Taunt lets Jellicent beat Amoonguss one-on-one by preventing Spore and outhealing Giga Drain damage with Recover; once Cursed Body activates, Amoonguss is incapable of doing anything to Jellicent.
  • Icy Wind can be used for another type of speed control.

Common Partners

  • Redirection users like Amoonguss all but ensure Trick Room goes up; Amoonguss can also incapacitate opposing Pokemon with Spore while Jellicent uses Water Spout.
  • Scrafty also helps Trick Room go up while threatening Pokemon that trouble Jellicent like Tyranitar, Cresselia, and opposing Jellicent.
  • Abomasnow is a useful secondary sweeper in Trick Room, as it checks Thundurus, Tornadus, and Amoonguss, which all give Jellicent problems.

Checks and Counters

  • Latios resists Water Spout and 2HKOes Jellicent with Psychic or Psyshock followed by Draco Meteor.
  • Tyranitar's Crunch OHKOes offensive sets and does considerable damage to even the bulkiest sets. Sand chip damage simultaneously reduces the longevity of Jellicent and the efficacy of Water Spout.
  • Amoonguss is not threatened by any of Jellicent's moves and can freely use Spore on either Jellicent or its partner. Amoonguss will more than likely move first under Trick Room and can ruin an attempt at sweeping during the precious few turns that Trick Room is active. However, a Cursed Body activation will prevent Amoonguss from damaging Jellicent.
  • Opposing bulky Water-types like Rotom-Wash, Suicune, and Gastrodon can all beat Jellicent in different ways: Rotom-Wash with Thunderbolt, Suicune with Snarl, and Gastrodon with Storm Drain. However, with Toxic and Recover, Jellicent can defeat all of these would-be counters.

Hydreigon.overview: Although slightly slower and weaker than Latios, Hydreigon's unique movepool and typing allow it to carve out a niche as a Dragon-type Pokemon capable of standing up to the common Steel-types of the format. In addition, Hydreigon's superior physical bulk allows it to survive a Flying Gem-boosted Acrobatics from Tornadus, a move that always OHKOes standard 4 HP Latios. While perhaps less plug-and-play compared to Latios, Hydreigon is equally threatening with Draco Meteor and uniquely dangerous with its potential coverage moves.

Special Sweeper.description:
• Hydreigon's powerful STAB moves give it a good matchup against most of the format, with Dragon Gem-boosted Draco Meteor greatly threatening anything it hits for neutral damage and Dark Pulse hitting two of the most common switch-ins, Metagross and Cresselia, for significant damage.
  • Coverage moves like Earth Power and Flamethrower give it a niche in its ability to beat common Steel-types such as Heatran, Metagross, and Scizor.
  • The 4 Defense EVs ensure Hydreigon always survives a Flying Gem-boosted Acrobatics from Tornadus.
  • Hydreigon's Speed tier allows it to beat most of the format and outspeed even some Pokemon with a higher base Speed that elect to run a non-Speed-boosting nature, most notably Volcarona and Latios.

Other Options

  • Hydreigon can sometimes run Tailwind to shift from a more offensive to supportive role on teams.
  • Hydreigon's natural bulk can also allow it to run Choice Specs or Choice Scarf, allowing it to surprise opponents with high damage or speed and flip the matchups against Pokemon like Latios, Cresselia, and Volcarona.

Common Partners

  • Cresselia can offer Hydreigon support or take down the Fighting-types that threaten it, while Hydreigon can deal with the Steel-types that Cresselia struggles to beat.
  • Metagross offers the same ability to beat Fighting-types as Cresselia while also resisting many of the hits from faster Pokemon that threaten Hydreigon, most notably Latios. In return, Hydreigon can obliterate many of the Pokemon Metagross doesn't want to face like Heatran.
  • Thundurus offers the best speed control for Hydreigon in Thunder Wave, letting it fire off attacks with no fear of being removed first.

Checks and Counters

  • Latios is faster and OHKOes Hydreigon with Draco Meteor.
  • Conkeldurr and Breloom 2HKO Hydreigon with Mach Punch. Conkeldurr needs only minimal chip damage to KO Hydreigon with a Life Orb-boosted Mach Punch, while Breloom survives any hit from Hydreigon thanks to its Focus Sash and always 2HKOs with Mach Punch.
  • Similarly, although Tornadus doesn't OHKO Hydreigon, any chip damage makes Hydreigon fodder for Tornadus.
  • Volcarona can be faster than Hydreigon and OHKOes it with Bug Buzz.

Tornadus.overview: Tornadus is a superb physical attacking option in VGC 2013. Thanks to its Defiant ability, Tornadus takes advantage of the omnipresent Intimidate Pokemon to get an Attack boost and sweep teams. In addition, Tornadus benefits from the damage stacking interaction of Flying Gem and Acrobatics, giving it one of the strongest attacks in the format. Flying has good neutral coverage, and it is easy to create a team that is weak to Tornadus, especially if it has a boost. Tornadus also has at its disposal a good enough support movepool, letting it enable its team or disrupt the enemy.

Defiant Attacker.description:
• Flying Gem-boosted Acrobatics OHKOes several top metagame threats such as Amoonguss, Conkeldurr, and Latios, and it deals significant damage when hitting for neutral damage.
  • The rest of the moveset is flexible and should be what most benefits the team. Superpower allows Tornadus to hit Flying-resistant foes for neutral damage, in the case of Rotom-Wash and Metagross, or super effective damage, in the case of Tyranitar and Heatran. U-turn is good for pivoting and scouting, Taunt can potentially shut down Trick Room, and Tailwind provides speed control.
  • A Jolly nature and maximum Speed EVs are required to outspeed Latios, while 252 Attack EVs are required to OHKO Latios with Acrobatics.

Other Options

  • Using the Prankster ability alongside Substitute, though it deals significantly less damage against teams with Intimidate, allows Tornadus to have a better matchup against Thundurus as well as Amoonguss when under Trick Room, blocking the status moves of both Pokemon before they can move.
  • Smack Down paired alongside Landorus-T makes for an effective turn one opening that beats Rotom-Wash and both formes of Thundurus.
  • Tornadus has a decent enough Special Attack that Heat Wave can be used to hit Pokemon like Scizor and Ferrothorn that are 4x weak to the move and would otherwise beat Tornadus.

Common Partners

  • Breloom and Tornadus form an offensive core, with both Pokemon threatening several top metagame threats which check the other; Breloom checks Tyranitar and Rotom-Wash while Tornadus checks Latios, for example. In addition, Breloom greatly appreciates the Tailwind that Tornadus can set, allowing it to use Spore to put almost every Pokemon to sleep before they can move. Although a dangerous pair, neither Breloom nor Tornadus threaten the omnipresent Thundurus, which checks both with Taunt and Thunderbolt. respectively.
  • Tornadus supports Landorus-T by checking several of its biggest threats, namely opposing Landorus-T. The pair have neutral or positive matchups against several common leads in the format.

Checks and Counters

  • Thundurus and Rotom-Wash resist Acrobatics. Thundurus will always OHKO with Thunderbolt, while Rotom-Wash can depending on its EV spread.
  • Even with Superpower, Tornadus does not particularly threaten the many Steel-types in the format.
  • Flying Gem-boosted Acrobatics from opposing Tornadus OHKOes, leaving the mirror matchup to be decided by Speed tie before accounting for outside intervention such as speed control or redirection.
